Summary:
Relationship structure: causal chain.

PU.1 (SPI1) specifies the macrophage/microglia lineage during development (mouse, zebrafish) and is required for embryonic macrophage and microglia generation—global disruption causes embryonic lethality and absence of these cells. In adults, PU.1/Spi1 dosage regulates microglia survival and long-term maintenance: complete loss (zebrafish Pu.1 with Spi‑b loss; mouse Spi1 Fl/Fl) causes rapid microglial depletion, whereas partial loss leads to chronic elimination only in mosaic contexts via cell competition. Mechanistically, Pu.1-deficient microglia show reduced proliferation and increased apoptosis accompanied by Tp53 upregulation, and Tp53 loss partially rescues pu.1-deficient cells in zebrafish. In zebrafish, Spi‑b can compensate during early development; Spi‑b is barely detectable in adult murine microglia. This dosage-dependent regulation is conserved from teleosts to mammals.
References: Wu, Y., Guo, W., Kuang, H., Wu, X., Trinh, T. H., Wang, Y., Zhao, S., Wen, Z., & Yu, T. (2025). Pu.1/Spi1 dosage controls the turnover and maintenance of microglia in zebrafish and mammals. Elife, 14, RP105788. https://doi.org/10.7554/elife.105788
